SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper runs extensive comparative experiments against 19 research tools across multiple datasets (A/B Italian and English app review sets and tweet sets), which supports the primary performance claims, but key methodological details are missing (no hyperparameters, undisclosed implementation/engine) limiting reproducibility. There are no reported ablation or sensitivity analyses to attribute gains to specific modeling choices, and code/models are not provided. Related work appears adequately covered and some limitations are acknowledged (e.g., non-disclosure, absence of neutral class in the English tweet set), but discussion of failure cases is minimal. Overall the experimental breadth is reasonable, yet the lack of ablations and reproduction details prevents a higher score. | B(3): The evaluation compares the tools against 19 research baselines across multiple datasets (Apps A/B in Italian/English and tweet corpora), which supports the primary performance claims reasonably well. However, there are important gaps: no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters and implementation details are undisclosed (hindering reproducibility), and the English tweet set lacks the neutral class. Related work coverage is said to be adequate and some limitations are noted, but the lack of reproducibility and in-depth error/failure analysis prevents a higher rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs extensive comparative experiments against 19 research tools across multiple datasets (A/B Italian and English app review sets and tweet sets), which supports the primary performance claims, but key methodological details are missing (no hyperparameters, undisclosed implementation/engine) limiting reproducibility. There are no reported ablation or sensitivity analyses to attribute gains to specific modeling choices, and code/models are not provided. Related work appears adequately covered and some limitations are acknowledged (e.g., non-disclosure, absence of neutral class in the English tweet set), but discussion of failure cases is minimal. Overall the experimental breadth is reasonable, yet the lack of ablations and reproduction details prevents a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The evaluation compares the tools against 19 research baselines across multiple datasets (Apps A/B in Italian/English and tweet corpora), which supports the primary performance claims reasonably well. However, there are important gaps: no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters and implementation details are undisclosed (hindering reproducibility), and the English tweet set lacks the neutral class. Related work coverage is said to be adequate and some limitations are noted, but the lack of reproducibility and in-depth error/failure analysis prevents a higher rating.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a large set of external baselines (19 SOTA systems via iFeel and SentiStrength) using the same test splits and standard metrics (macro-F1, accuracy and per-class F1), which is appropriate. However, important reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters, core implementation undisclosed) and one evaluation set (English tweets) lacks the neutral class, which complicates direct comparison; the paper also does not clearly analyze which components drive the gains versus prior work. Related work is otherwise adequately covered, so I rate the comparisons as good with notable minor omissions. | B(4): The paper compares against a large set of baselines (19 research tools via the iFeel platform and SentiStrengthIta) using the same test sets and standard metrics (MF1, Acc, per-class F1), which is strong. However, some fairness concerns remain: implementation/engine details and hyperparameters are undisclosed, the English tweet test set lacks the neutral class, and it is unclear whether baselines were adapted or retrained for the exact domains—issues that could bias comparisons. The manuscript appears to discuss related work adequately but gives limited analysis of when prior methods perform similarly or better and of why its models outperform them.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a large set of external baselines (19 SOTA systems via iFeel and SentiStrength) using the same test splits and standard metrics (macro-F1, accuracy and per-class F1), which is appropriate. However, important reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters, core implementation undisclosed) and one evaluation set (English tweets) lacks the neutral class, which complicates direct comparison; the paper also does not clearly analyze which components drive the gains versus prior work. Related work is otherwise adequately covered, so I rate the comparisons as good with notable minor omissions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a large set of baselines (19 research tools via the iFeel platform and SentiStrengthIta) using the same test sets and standard metrics (MF1, Acc, per-class F1), which is strong. However, some fairness concerns remain: implementation/engine details and hyperparameters are undisclosed, the English tweet test set lacks the neutral class, and it is unclear whether baselines were adapted or retrained for the exact domains—issues that could bias comparisons. The manuscript appears to discuss related work adequately but gives limited analysis of when prior methods perform similarly or better and of why its models outperform them.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear, testable claims and reports empirical comparisons on multiple test sets (A-ita/A-eng 1k each, B-ita/B-eng 10k each, plus tweet sets) against 19 research tools via iFeel and SentiStrength, which supports the main empirical claims. However, important reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters, training procedures, or code, and model/engine details withheld), and there is no mention of statistical significance testing or ablation to attribute gains to specific design choices. The English tweet set lacking a neutral class and undisclosed implementation choices create logical gaps that weaken the strength of the conclusions despite the reported metrics. Overall, the experimental evidence is promising but incomplete for full technical validation. | B(3): The paper presents clear experimental comparisons to a broad set of baselines (19 tools via iFeel and SentiStrengthIta) on multiple labeled test sets in Italian and English, and uses appropriate metrics (macro-F1, per-class F1), which supports the performance claims to an extent. However, critical methodological details are missing (model/engine implementation, hyperparameters, and no mention of statistical significance testing), and one of the English tweet test sets lacks the neutral class—issues that limit reproducibility and raise concerns about the robustness of reported gains. There are no mathematical proofs to assess, but the undisclosed implementation and absent experimental controls are notable gaps that prevent full technical validation of the claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and reports empirical comparisons on multiple test sets (A-ita/A-eng 1k each, B-ita/B-eng 10k each, plus tweet sets) against 19 research tools via iFeel and SentiStrength, which supports the main empirical claims. However, important reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters, training procedures, or code, and model/engine details withheld), and there is no mention of statistical significance testing or ablation to attribute gains to specific design choices. The English tweet set lacking a neutral class and undisclosed implementation choices create logical gaps that weaken the strength of the conclusions despite the reported metrics. Overall, the experimental evidence is promising but incomplete for full technical validation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents clear experimental comparisons to a broad set of baselines (19 tools via iFeel and SentiStrengthIta) on multiple labeled test sets in Italian and English, and uses appropriate metrics (macro-F1, per-class F1), which supports the performance claims to an extent. However, critical methodological details are missing (model/engine implementation, hyperparameters, and no mention of statistical significance testing), and one of the English tweet test sets lacks the neutral class—issues that limit reproducibility and raise concerns about the robustness of reported gains. There are no mathematical proofs to assess, but the undisclosed implementation and absent experimental controls are notable gaps that prevent full technical validation of the claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper addresses a well-studied problem (sentiment analysis of reviews and tweets) using supervised predictive models and standard NLP modules (topic/NER), trained on particular app- and tweet-specific datasets; this is largely an application of existing techniques rather than a new method. The authors do compare extensively to many baselines (iFeel/SentiStrength and 19 tools), but they do not disclose novel algorithmic contributions, hyperparameters, or implementation details—suggesting limited methodological innovation. From my methodological-reproducer perspective, the work reads as combining known supervised approaches and auxiliary NLP components for a particular domain rather than introducing a non-trivial extension that would surprise experts. | B(2): The paper addresses a well-studied problem (sentiment analysis/quantification of tweets and app reviews) using supervised models and standard NLP components (topic/NER), and does not disclose novel algorithmic details. Its main contribution appears to be application-specific engineering and reported performance gains on curated Italian/English datasets rather than a new method or theoretical perspective. Given the lack of disclosed methodological innovation and reliance on comparisons to existing tools (iFeel, SentiStrength), the contribution reads as limited novelty beyond combining established techniques for particular domains.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (sentiment analysis of reviews and tweets) using supervised predictive models and standard NLP modules (topic/NER), trained on particular app- and tweet-specific datasets; this is largely an application of existing techniques rather than a new method. The authors do compare extensively to many baselines (iFeel/SentiStrength and 19 tools), but they do not disclose novel algorithmic contributions, hyperparameters, or implementation details—suggesting limited methodological innovation. From my methodological-reproducer perspective, the work reads as combining known supervised approaches and auxiliary NLP components for a particular domain rather than introducing a non-trivial extension that would surprise experts.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (sentiment analysis/quantification of tweets and app reviews) using supervised models and standard NLP components (topic/NER), and does not disclose novel algorithmic details. Its main contribution appears to be application-specific engineering and reported performance gains on curated Italian/English datasets rather than a new method or theoretical perspective. Given the lack of disclosed methodological innovation and reliance on comparisons to existing tools (iFeel, SentiStrength), the contribution reads as limited novelty beyond combining established techniques for particular domains.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): Important training/configuration details are missing: no hyperparameters, model architectures, or training procedures are reported and the authors explicitly withhold implementation/engine details. The data situation is mixed—tweet source partly public (Araújo et al.) but the app-review test sets are described only by size/content and may not be publicly released, and preprocessing is not specified. Code is not provided and reimplementation would therefore require many assumptions. Evaluation metrics are clearly reported (macro‑F1, accuracy, per‑class F1), which aids comparability but is insufficient to enable full reproduction. | B(2): From a reproducibility standpoint the paper is difficult to reproduce: implementation/engine details and hyperparameters are explicitly undisclosed and no code is reported, which prevents reimplementation of the trained systems. The data situation is mixed—tweet data partly stems from a public source but the app-review corpora are not clearly stated as public or fully described for exact recreation, and the preprocessing pipeline is not detailed. Evaluation metrics and test splits are described, which helps, but key training/configuration and processing details are missing, so independent replication would be hard.

reviewer_a.justification: Important training/configuration details are missing: no hyperparameters, model architectures, or training procedures are reported and the authors explicitly withhold implementation/engine details. The data situation is mixed—tweet source partly public (Araújo et al.) but the app-review test sets are described only by size/content and may not be publicly released, and preprocessing is not specified. Code is not provided and reimplementation would therefore require many assumptions. Evaluation metrics are clearly reported (macro‑F1, accuracy, per‑class F1), which aids comparability but is insufficient to enable full reproduction.

reviewer_b.justification: From a reproducibility standpoint the paper is difficult to reproduce: implementation/engine details and hyperparameters are explicitly undisclosed and no code is reported, which prevents reimplementation of the trained systems. The data situation is mixed—tweet data partly stems from a public source but the app-review corpora are not clearly stated as public or fully described for exact recreation, and the preprocessing pipeline is not detailed. Evaluation metrics and test splits are described, which helps, but key training/configuration and processing details are missing, so independent replication would be hard.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The topic—supervised sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets—is squarely within a top NLP/ML venue and the work is evaluated with standard metrics (macro-F1, per-class F1, accuracy) against a strong set of 19 research baselines. However, important reproducibility details are missing (implementation/engine details undisclosed, no hyperparameters or code release) and one test set lacks the neutral class, which weaken methodological transparency even though dataset sizes and baseline comparisons are otherwise appropriate. Overall this is a good fit for the venue but would benefit from fuller experimental disclosure to meet top-conference standards. | B(3): The paper addresses an NLP-relevant problem (sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets) and presents multilingual, applied tools evaluated against many baselines on multiple datasets, which appeals to practitioner and applied-NLP audiences. However, key methodological details are undisclosed and the contribution reads as an engineering/tool deployment with limited methodological novelty, making it a better fit for an applications or demo track rather than a main conference research track. Overall, it is borderline appropriate for a top-tier venue unless positioned with clearer scientific contributions or released implementation/details.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—supervised sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets—is squarely within a top NLP/ML venue and the work is evaluated with standard metrics (macro-F1, per-class F1, accuracy) against a strong set of 19 research baselines. However, important reproducibility details are missing (implementation/engine details undisclosed, no hyperparameters or code release) and one test set lacks the neutral class, which weaken methodological transparency even though dataset sizes and baseline comparisons are otherwise appropriate. Overall this is a good fit for the venue but would benefit from fuller experimental disclosure to meet top-conference standards.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an NLP-relevant problem (sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets) and presents multilingual, applied tools evaluated against many baselines on multiple datasets, which appeals to practitioner and applied-NLP audiences. However, key methodological details are undisclosed and the contribution reads as an engineering/tool deployment with limited methodological novelty, making it a better fit for an applications or demo track rather than a main conference research track. Overall, it is borderline appropriate for a top-tier venue unless positioned with clearer scientific contributions or released implementation/details.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized with a clear flow from introduction through methods and experiments, and the main contributions (two tools and their performance claims) are stated early and repeatedly. Notation and presentation appear consistent and readable, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective in supporting results. Minor clarity issues stem from undisclosed implementation/hyperparameter details (noted as non-disclosure) which reduce reproducibility rather than immediate readability, and a few sections could benefit from more explicit dataset/split descriptions to aid methodical reproduction. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (App2Check/Tweet2Check, languages/domains, 1–5 scoring and additional NLP modules) are stated early and consistently. Notation and writing are generally clear and readable, and figures/tables are reported as adequate and informative for the experiments across the multiple test sets. Presentation is weakened by omission of implementation/engine details (non-disclosure) and missing hyperparameter reporting, which reduce reproducibility and leave some experimental decisions opaque. These are relatively minor presentation issues in an otherwise clear manuscript.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized with a clear flow from introduction through methods and experiments, and the main contributions (two tools and their performance claims) are stated early and repeatedly. Notation and presentation appear consistent and readable, and figures/tables/examples are reported as effective in supporting results. Minor clarity issues stem from undisclosed implementation/hyperparameter details (noted as non-disclosure) which reduce reproducibility rather than immediate readability, and a few sections could benefit from more explicit dataset/split descriptions to aid methodical reproduction.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (App2Check/Tweet2Check, languages/domains, 1–5 scoring and additional NLP modules) are stated early and consistently. Notation and writing are generally clear and readable, and figures/tables are reported as adequate and informative for the experiments across the multiple test sets. Presentation is weakened by omission of implementation/engine details (non-disclosure) and missing hyperparameter reporting, which reduce reproducibility and leave some experimental decisions opaque. These are relatively minor presentation issues in an otherwise clear manuscript.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles a practical and relevant problem—sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets in English and Italian—and demonstrates empirical gains over 19 baseline tools on sizable test sets (10k app comments per language and separate tweet corpora), which makes the contribution useful for practitioners. However, the undisclosed implementation/hyperparameter details and absence of released code reduce reproducibility and limit how readily researchers can adopt or build on the approach, tempering its broader impact. While the results could influence applied sentiment-analysis pipelines for these domains and languages, they are unlikely to change underlying research directions or be transformative without clearer methodological transparency. | B(3): The paper tackles a relevant practical problem—accurate sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets in both Italian and English—and reports strong empirical gains over 19 research baselines, which makes it immediately interesting to practitioners monitoring app UX and social media. However, undisclosed implementation details and missing reproducibility artifacts limit its influence on researchers and slower uptake for new methodological work. The bilingual, domain-specific tools and inclusion of topic/NER components increase real-world applicability, but the contribution appears more engineering- and evaluation-focused than likely to open major new research directions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a practical and relevant problem—sentiment analysis of app reviews and tweets in English and Italian—and demonstrates empirical gains over 19 baseline tools on sizable test sets (10k app comments per language and separate tweet corpora), which makes the contribution useful for practitioners. However, the undisclosed implementation/hyperparameter details and absence of released code reduce reproducibility and limit how readily researchers can adopt or build on the approach, tempering its broader impact. While the results could influence applied sentiment-analysis pipelines for these domains and languages, they are unlikely to change underlying research directions or be transformative without clearer methodological transparency.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a relevant practical problem—accurate sentiment quantification for app reviews and tweets in both Italian and English—and reports strong empirical gains over 19 research baselines, which makes it immediately interesting to practitioners monitoring app UX and social media. However, undisclosed implementation details and missing reproducibility artifacts limit its influence on researchers and slower uptake for new methodological work. The bilingual, domain-specific tools and inclusion of topic/NER components increase real-world applicability, but the contribution appears more engineering- and evaluation-focused than likely to open major new research directions.

main_claims: App2Check and Tweet2Check are supervised learning-based tools for sentiment quantification of app reviews and tweets in Italian and English.
App2Check outperforms state-of-the-art research competitors on apps reviews.
Tweet2Check outperforms state-of-the-art research competitors on tweets in both Italian and English.
The tools provide a 1–5 sentiment polarity scoring (mapped to negative/neutral/positive) and include NLP algorithms such as topic/named-entity extraction.
_summary.methodology: Two supervised learning predictive models were trained (one specialized for app reviews, one for tweets) on a variety of language domains and user reviews; final answers use additional NLP algorithms (e.g., topic/NER) alongside the models (implementation details not disclosed).
datasets: Test set A-ita (1000 Italian Candy Crush Saga comments, manually annotated HSQ)
Test set A-eng (1000 English Candy Crush Saga comments, manually annotated HSQ)
Test set B-ita (10,000 Italian comments: 10 apps, 1000 each)
Test set B-eng (10,000 English comments: 10 apps, 1000 each)
Italian tweets (3899 tweets from Araújo et al., 2016/Sem 2016)
English tweets (1000 tweets randomly selected from same source)
baselines: 19 state-of-the-art research tools via the iFeel platform
iFeel platform outputs (used for both Italian and English)
metrics: per-class F1 (F1(-), F1(x) if neutral present, F1(+))
limitations: Implementation/engine details are not disclosed due to non-disclosure restrictions.
The English tweet test set does not contain the neutral class (explicitly stated).
Human agreement on sentiment is known to be around 80% (noted as reference), implying an upper bound on performance comparisons.

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Reproducer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher with 10+ years designing and evaluating supervised NLP systems, with prior experience releasing reproducible toolkits for sentiment analysis and applied text classification.
traits: insists on clear descriptions of datasets (sizes, splits, preprocessing) and training procedures to assess reproducibility
requires strong experimental baselines, hyperparameter details, and ablation studies to attribute gains to specific design choices
checks for appropriate evaluation metrics, statistical significance testing, and robustness across domains and label distributions
looks for open-sourcing of code/models or, if not possible, sufficient implementation details to replicate results
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on methodological rigor and reproducibility: will dissect experimental setup, metrics, baselines, and whether the claims are supported by the experiments.

reviewer_b.name: Applied NLP Landscapeer
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP with a track record of research on sentiment analysis, multilingual systems, and industrial application of text-mining tools; familiar with user-facing analytics platforms and related literature.
traits: evaluates novelty and positioning relative to prior work on sentiment quantification and multilingual sentiment tools
values practical impact, usability, and whether the tool addresses real needs (e.g., granularity of scores, domain adaptation, multilingual support)
notices missing comparisons to widely-used industry baselines or deployment constraints (e.g., runtime, annotation costs, privacy)
draws attention to how well the work generalizes across languages, register (tweets vs app reviews), and evolving language use
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the paper's novelty, applicability, and significance in context of the field and industry needs, with attention to what practitioners would care about.
